questions.question: How many levels of headings does HTML provide?
questions.explanation: HTML provides six heading levels, from <h1> (most important) to <h6> (least important). These create a hierarchical structure for document content.

questions.question: Which heading tag represents the highest level of importance?
questions.explanation: The <h1> tag represents the most important heading. Heading importance decreases from <h1> to <h6>, with <h1> typically used for the main page title.

questions.question: Why is it considered best practice to have only one <h1> per page?
choices: Browsers will not render more than one <h1>
It improves SEO and provides a clear document structure
The HTML specification forbids multiple <h1> tags
questions.explanation: Using a single <h1> per page helps search engines understand the main topic. It provides a clear hierarchy and improves accessibility for screen readers.

questions.question: What is the correct practice when using heading levels?
choices: Skip levels freely for visual effect
Use headings in sequential order without skipping levels
questions.explanation: Headings should follow a logical hierarchy without skipping levels (e.g., h1 then h2, not h1 then h4). This ensures proper document structure and accessibility.

questions.question: The ___ tag is the smallest and least important heading level in HTML.
questions.explanation: The <h6> tag is the lowest-level heading in HTML. It renders in the smallest default font size among headings and represents the least significant section heading.

questions.question: Heading tags are important for ___ because screen readers use them to navigate page structure.
questions.explanation: Screen readers rely on heading tags to build a navigable outline of the page. Users can jump between headings to quickly find the content they need.
